summary.overall: Chapter 13 was reviewed in 6 bounded source-grounded shards. Proposed changes: 67 replacements, 4 splits, and 6 removals.
patterns: Paragraph-location scaffolding in all 11 question stems and explanations — stems begin with 'Under 13-1-X' and explanations cite 'Per 13-1-X' or 'the note in 13-1-X' rather than teaching the operational principle.

Thin explanations in two true/false items — explanations say 'That statement appears directly in [paragraph]' without teaching anything. Both true/false items are redundant with stronger MCQ or flashcard coverage of the same content.

Four of five activities are near-duplicate knowledge_checks of corresponding MCQs — identical or nearly identical stems and answer concepts, offering no additional retrieval mode or operational context.

The one situation_action activity (13-1-12 overdue aircraft) is well-constructed with a realistic scenario, plausible distractors, and an explanation that teaches the operational principle. Its negative_stem automated flag is a false positive from the heuristic.
Flashcards are uniformly strong — self-contained fronts, accurate and concise backs, appropriate card types. None require intervention.

Two source elements are untested: EDST's prediction of conflicts between aircraft and special use/designated airspace (13-1-1 body), and the specific prohibition against using trajectory-based positions as a substitute for radar track position (13-1-13b).

Paragraph-location scaffolding is systemic: 10 of 11 question stems or explanations embed 'Under 13-1-X' or 'Per 13-1-X(e)' prefixes, sometimes on both fields. Removing these prefixes and capitalising the first word consistently resolves the issue without altering substantitve content.

One overloaded flashcard (1583a427) combines a definition list of three Airspace Configuration Elements with the sector-team duties for those elements. The two retrieval targets interfere with each other and should be separate cards.

One cross-format duplication: activity b434ef41 (knowledge_check) asks the same question as question b8f268ce (multiple_choice) with near-identical answer choices. Both test recall of the three Airspace Configuration Elements from 13-1-17, wasting a retrieval opportunity on this content-rich paragraph.

The two situation-action activities (b4dbc6a4 and 68d178f0) are well-constructed with realistic diverging-controller-opinion scenarios, plausible distractors, and operationally sound explanations. Their automated negative_stem flags are false positives for this activity type.
Flashcards f7b1e23a, 766250b6, 705cd12e, and 8e5d614e are each concise, single-target, and procedurally accurate. They need no intervention.

True-false question 9588bdb5 has a thin explanation that merely locates the statement in the source instead of teaching the operational principle; the fix replaces it with an explanation that enumerates all four trial-planning uses while confirming the specific one tested.

Paragraph-location scaffolding is pervasive across all 11 questions and 3 activity content objects. Every question stem begins with 'Under 13-1-X', and many explanations state 'Per 13-1-X' or 'That statement appears in 13-1-X' instead of teaching the controlling rule. This trains location trivia over operational substance.
Thin explanations are the second-most common defect. Five questions and two activities explain only by restating the answer or pointing to the paragraph number, providing no instructional value.
Answer-length cues affect three questions where the correct answer is the only multi-clause choice, creating a structural tell independent of content knowledge.

Concept redundancy: 13-1-5's knowledge_check activity (cc4a9fed) duplicates question b9b83819 on the ACL-as-primary-source concept, and 13-1-6's knowledge_check activity (a4aaa698) duplicates question 41c7788c on yellow coding removal — both in the same multiple-choice retrieval mode.
All four flashcards are clean and well-formed with no automated flags. Their front/back pairs are concise, self-contained, and faithful to the source.
Negative stems flagged on three questions and one activity stem from the source's own conditional language ('not available') or from testing the rule's exception, which is educationally legitimate.
The two situation_action activities (ce8f8ad8, 7afe66c0) provide good operational scenarios that contextualize the rule.

Paragraph-location scaffolding is the dominant defect — 11 of 12 questions and 2 of 5 activities cite paragraph numbers in stems, explanations, or para_context, substituting document trivia for operational substance.
Two questions (1f3a710f, dc3fcf3c) pack multiple distinct procedural requirements into one item, creating answer-length cues and educational overload.
One flashcard (62baaa4c) dumps three separate operational rules into a single back, violating the one-retrieval-target-per-card standard.
Three true/false questions have explanations under 10 words that merely repeat which paragraph contains the answer rather than teaching the rule.
Both situation-action activities correctly frame incorrect-controller-behavior scenarios, but their para_context fields open with paragraph citations that should be removed.
The table-lookup activity (ecdb2522) and knowledge-check activity (9a2b1488) are self-contained and well-formatted — no intervention needed.
Three flashcards (cb6502cf, 928ac668, e035a8ca) are focused, accurate, and educationally sound.
Every question stem opens with 'Under 13-2-X' paragraph-location scaffolding that substitutes document navigation for operational substance.
Multiple question explanations merely cite a paragraph identifier instead of teaching the controlling operational rule.
Several true/false questions carry explanations that restate the answer (e.g., 'That statement appears directly in 13-2-4c5') without teaching the underlying principle.
One flashcard (b8408735) overloads its back with annotations and restrictions content beyond the front's question about reduced-separation flags.
Activity 44cce207 embeds a paragraph-location reference in its para_context field.
Correct-answer length is notably longer than distractor lengths in two multiple-choice items (d41b4db5, fc26c32b), creating a surface cue independent of content knowledge.
The remaining three activities and three flashcards are well-constructed, self-contained, and test operational decision-making without scaffolding.

Paragraph-location scaffolding in all three question stems and explanations — stems begin with 'Under 13-2-7' and explanations cite 'Per 13-2-7a', 'Per 13-2-7b', or 'because 13-2-7 requires', teaching paragraph-number recall instead of the operational substance.

Unsupported 'ATOP workstation' specificity in the activity and flashcard — the source block's rule text for 13-2-7 does not mention ATOP; the word appears only in page-header navigation text. Items should use the source's own phrasing ('multiple controller operation at a workstation').

Length cue in the activity — the correct choice combines both sub-paragraphs into one sentence, making it substantially longer and more detailed than any single distractor. Distractors need comparable heft.

Informal flashcard wording — the back uses 'still have to be completed' instead of the source's 'must be completed' or 'shall be completed,' weakening the authoritative tone expected for FAA procedure recall.
Content bandwidth is narrow — five items all test the same two rules without operational scenario depth, but this reflects the thin source paragraph rather than a generation defect.
generation_guidance: Strip all paragraph-number references from stems and explanations. Every question must be answerable from operational knowledge without knowing which paragraph a rule lives in.

Replace true/false items that restate verbatim source sentences with multiple-choice questions testing operational recognition or application. Reserve true/false for exclusion-recognition scenarios where identifying a false statement requires operational judgment.

Provide explanations that teach the controlling rule's content, scope, and operational conditions — not merely that 'the source says this.' When a prohibition is tested, explain what IS permitted alongside what is not.

Add question coverage for 13-1-1 conflict prediction targets: EDST predicts conflicts between aircraft and between aircraft and special use/designated airspace. Also test the trial planning and enhanced flight data management capabilities mentioned in the body but not covered by any item.

Add question coverage for 13-1-13b: trajectory-based positions from the GPD must not be used as a substitute for radar track position. This is a distinct prohibition from 13-1-13c (Mode C substitute) and deserves independent testing.

When generating activities, avoid creating knowledge_checks that duplicate existing multiple-choice questions. Use situation_action, table_lookup, or source_use activity types to reinforce the same operational concepts through different retrieval modes.

Cross-format reinforcement is currently weak in this shard — each paragraph has at most two formats covering a concept. Add activities for 13-1-11 (delay recording) and 13-1-14 (forecast wind contingency) to strengthen multi-format coverage.
Do not embed paragraph numbers in question stems or explanations. Items must be self-contained; the source reference belongs in metadata or a separate source_basis field, not in learner-facing text.

When a paragraph has three or more distinct operational concepts (as 13-1-17 does with Airspace Configuration Elements, sector duty to update/notify, and SAA coordination limitation), distribute activity types across those concepts rather than repeating the same concept in both a question and a knowledge-check activity.

Flashcards should target exactly one retrieval item. When a paragraph combines a list with associated duties, split them: one card for the list, one for the duty. Avoid combining two question marks on one card.

True-false explanations must teach the controlling rule, not merely confirm that the statement appears in the source. For a true-false item testing a sub-element of a list, the explanation should contextualize it within the full list so the learner sees the broader operational framework.

Continue using situation-action activities for conditional and coordination-heavy procedures (such as the 13-1-15 connectivity-loss scenario and the 13-1-16 outage scenario). These are the strongest activity type in this shard and model effective operational-decision training.

When an automated flag fires on a situation-action activity for negative_stem, inspect the activity before acting. Situation-action activities frame dilemmas differently from questions and the negative-stem heuristic does not apply to them.
Remove all paragraph-number references from stems and explanations. Teach the operational rule, condition, or procedure directly — never the paragraph location.

When a single paragraph has only one core operational statement, generate one question and one flashcard or one question and one activity — not both a question and a knowledge_check activity in the same retrieval mode.
Match distractor length and specificity to the correct answer. When the correct answer is a list or multi-part statement, make distractors comparably specific.
For T/F questions based on single-sentence source rules, ensure the explanation highlights the controlling principle rather than merely confirming the paragraph number.
Flashcards in this shard are the strongest format. Continue the pattern of concise, self-contained front prompts with operational back answers.
Never embed paragraph numbers in question stems, explanations, or activity context fields. Stems must be self-contained operational prompts.
Do not combine multiple distinct procedural requirements into a single question. One question tests one rule or one operational decision.
Flashcard backs must target one retrieval concept. If a paragraph contains several independent duties, create separate cards.
True/false explanations must teach the controlling rule, not merely cite the source paragraph. Minimum 10 substantive words explaining the operational requirement.
Situation-action activities may describe incorrect controller behavior to set up the scenario, but the para_context field must not contain paragraph-location scaffolding.
For ERT-related items (13-1-9f), test the first-sector handling rule and the track-control authority rule as separate retrieval targets.
For Free Text Area items (13-1-8d/e), keep the visibility requirement and the update/delete requirement as distinct retrieval targets.

Strip all paragraph-location references from question stems, activity prompt fields (including para_context), and explanations. Operational knowledge must be self-contained without citing a paragraph number.

Every explanation must teach the controlling operational principle with enough source detail that the student understands the rule, its conditions, and its operational context — not merely reiterate the answer or point to a paragraph.

Balance correct-answer text length against distractor lengths in multiple-choice items. When the correct answer is a compound duty with three or more elements, either trim it to essentials or expand distractors to similar word counts so length alone does not signal the answer.

Flashcard backs must confine themselves to the concept the front asks about. Material from a different sub-paragraph (e.g., annotations and restrictions on the same card as reduced-separation flags) belongs on a separate card or is best omitted if already covered.
True/false explanations should teach why the statement is true or false by stating the governing rule and any relevant exceptions. A bare paragraph pointer is not an explanation.

The automated flag 'negative_stem' on activity 44cce207 is spurious; the situation-action format presents a scenario, not a negated stem. Automated heuristics should be reviewed before flagging situation-based activities.
Do not add contextual specificity (e.g., 'ATOP') to stems or flashcards unless the source block's rule text itself contains that term. Use the source's own operational phrasing.

When a correct multiple-choice answer combines multiple sub-paragraphs, ensure distractors are comparably detailed to avoid length cues. Each distractor should match the grammatical structure and approximate length of the correct answer.
For flashcards covering FAA procedures, use the source's imperative verb forms ('must,' 'shall,' 'ensure') rather than colloquial equivalents ('have to,' 'still have to').

For paragraphs as thin as 13-2-7, consider whether items can operationalize the rules rather than merely restating them — e.g., a scenario question where a controller must decide whether to defer a task based on priority, or whether a team's ad-hoc arrangement violates facility directives.
shard_overviews: This shard covers six paragraphs (13-1-1, 13-1-10 through 13-1-14) spanning EDST description, trajectory currency, delay reporting, overdue aircraft, GPD usage, and forecast wind procedures. Source fidelity is uniformly high — all items correctly represent the source material with no factual errors. The dominant defect across questions is paragraph-location scaffolding: 8 of 11 question stems begin with 'Under 13-1-X' and all 11 question explanations cite paragraph numbers, teaching document navigation rather than operational knowledge. Two true/false items (13-1-1 and 13-1-13) have thin explanations that merely state the source contains the sentence, and their tested concepts are already covered by stronger items. Four of five activities are near-duplicates of corresponding multiple-choice questions with identical stems and concepts; only the 13-1-12 situation_action activity is well-constructed and distinct from its question counterpart. All six flashcards are well-formed with self-contained fronts, accurate backs, and appropriate card types; none require intervention. Content gaps exist: 13-1-1 conflict prediction targets (aircraft-aircraft and aircraft-special-use-airspace) are not tested by any item, and 13-1-13b (trajectory positions must not substitute for radar track) lacks independent question coverage.

This shard covers five paragraphs of technical ERAM procedure material and is broadly accurate. Source fidelity is strong across all formats, and no item teaches incorrect operational content. The consistent defect is paragraph-location scaffolding in stems and explanations, affecting 10 of 11 questions across all five paragraphs. One flashcard is overloaded with two retrieval targets, and one knowledge-check activity duplicates a multiple-choice question testing the same concept in a near-identical retrieval mode. The two situation-action activities and four standalone flashcards are well-constructed and require no intervention. No items require removal; all teach valid operational substance.

This shard covers four short paragraphs (13-1-4 through 13-1-7) with 19 target items. The flashcards are well-formed with no defects. Every question exhibits paragraph-location scaffolding in the stem, and many explanations reference paragraph numbers rather than teaching the operational rule. Two knowledge_check activities duplicate questions on the same concept in the same multiple-choice retrieval mode. Several questions exhibit answer-length cues where the correct choice is markedly longer than distractors. After remediation, content quality is solid — the items cover distinct operational rules with appropriate retrieval diversity across formats.

This shard covers four paragraphs (13-1-8 through 13-2-2) with 21 items. The most pervasive defect is paragraph-location scaffolding: nearly every question stem begins with "Under 13-X-X", and several explanations cite paragraph numbers instead of teaching the rule. Two questions and one flashcard are overloaded with multiple distinct retrieval targets packed into a single item. A few true/false questions have explanations as thin as "That statement appears directly in 13-X-Xa" that do not teach the controlling principle. The activities, table-lookup items, and most flashcards are generally well-constructed once scaffolding is removed. One knowledge-check activity and one situation-action activity have paragraph references in their para_context fields. The table-lookup activity (ecdb2522) correctly identifies its source table and asks a lookup decision — its thin explanation is acceptable for a rote abbreviation-memory item.

This shard covers paragraphs 13-2-3 through 13-2-6 of the ATOP Oceanic chapter. Item quality is structurally adequate but systematically undermined by paragraph-location scaffolding in every question stem and most question explanations. All four source paragraphs are represented with cross-format reinforcement on key operational concepts: CPDLC airspace protection, weather deviation pink advisories, and automated coordination exceptions. Activities are generally well-constructed with plausible distractors and operational scenarios. Once scaffolding is removed and thin explanations are replaced, the items test genuine operational knowledge rather than document navigation.

This shard covers a single thin paragraph (13-2-7) with two short rules about multiple-controller operation: tasks must follow priority of duties, and the operation must follow facility directives. Source fidelity is high — no item misrepresents the source content. The dominant defect is paragraph-location scaffolding: all three question stems begin with 'Under 13-2-7' and all three explanations cite paragraph numbers, teaching document navigation rather than operational knowledge. The activity and flashcard share an unsupported 'ATOP workstation' reference not present in the paragraph's rule text, and the flashcard uses informal phrasing ('still have to be'). The activity's correct answer is noticeably longer than its distractors, creating a length cue. With only two source sentences feeding five items, cross-format coverage is present but the content bandwidth is narrow — each format variant tests the same pair of rules without deeper operational application.

operations.problem: The stem begins 'Under 13-1-1' and the explanation cites 'Per the note in 13-1-1,' teaching paragraph-location trivia instead of the operational definition that the current plan is what EAS predicts the aircraft will fly, potentially including unissued clearances.
operations.source_basis: For use by the EDST, the current plan is what the En Route Automation System (EAS) predicts an aircraft will fly. This may include clearances that have not yet been issued to the aircraft. Current plans are used to model a flight trajectory and, when applicable, for detecting conflicts.

match.question_text: Under 13-1-1, for EDST use, what is the current plan?

replacements.question_text: For EDST use, what is the current plan?
replacements.explanation: The current plan is what the En Route Automation System (EAS) predicts an aircraft will fly. It may include clearances that have not yet been issued to the aircraft, and is used to model flight trajectories and detect conflicts.

choices.text: What EAS predicts an aircraft will fly, which may include clearances not yet issued to the aircraft.
Only the last clearance actually acknowledged by the pilot, with no unissued changes included.
The route and altitude in the original flight plan, regardless of later amendments or predictions.
The traffic management initiative currently in effect for the sector.

operations.problem: The stem begins 'Under 13-1-1' and the explanation cites 'Per 13-1-1,' teaching paragraph-location trivia instead of the operational knowledge of what data EDST uses to derive aircraft trajectories.
operations.source_basis: EDST uses current plan data, forecast winds, aircraft performance characteristics, and track data to derive expected aircraft trajectories, and to predict conflicts between aircraft and between aircraft and special use or designated airspace.

match.question_text: Under 13-1-1, which data set does EDST use to derive expected aircraft trajectories?

replacements.question_text: Which data set does EDST use to derive expected aircraft trajectories?
replacements.explanation: EDST uses current plan data, forecast winds, aircraft performance characteristics, and track data to derive expected aircraft trajectories. These same inputs also support conflict prediction between aircraft and between aircraft and special use or designated airspace.

choices.text: Current plan data, forecast winds, aircraft performance characteristics, and track data.
Only filed route and altitude information from the original flight plan.
Only current radar position plus pilot weather reports.
Tower departure messages and runway assignment data only.

operations.problem: The true/false stem begins 'Under 13-1-1' and the explanation states only 'That statement appears directly in the note to 13-1-1,' providing zero educational value. The tested concept (current plan includes unissued clearances) is already covered by question eb4d62c9. Additionally, the source body's statement that EDST predicts conflicts between aircraft and special use/designated airspace is not tested by any item in this shard.
operations.source_basis: EDST uses current plan data, forecast winds, aircraft performance characteristics, and track data to derive expected aircraft trajectories, and to predict conflicts between aircraft and between aircraft and special use or designated airspace.

match.question_text: Under 13-1-1, EDST's current plan may include clearances that have not yet been issued to the aircraft.

replacements.question_text: What types of conflicts does EDST predict?
replacements.explanation: EDST predicts conflicts between aircraft and between aircraft and special use or designated airspace. It uses current plan data, forecast winds, aircraft performance characteristics, and track data as inputs for both trajectory derivation and conflict prediction.

choices.text: Conflicts between aircraft and between aircraft and special use or designated airspace.
Only loss-of-separation conflicts between two aircraft at the same altitude.
Only airspace-penetration conflicts for restricted and prohibited areas.
Only potential conflicts along the filed route, disregarding amended clearances.

operations.problem: The explanation begins 'Per 13-1-10,' citing a paragraph location instead of teaching the operational principle that stale trajectory data produces misleading conflict-probe notifications for both the subject sector and surrounding facilities.
operations.source_basis: Conflict probe accuracy requires timely updates of data used to model each flight's trajectory. If this data is not current, the aircraft entries and notification of probe results for surrounding sectors and facilities, as well as the subject sector, may be misleading.

match.question_text: Why must automation entries be made in EDST without delay?

replacements.question_text: Why must automation entries be made in EDST without delay?
replacements.explanation: Conflict-probe accuracy depends on current trajectory data such as route of flight, assigned and interim altitudes, adapted restrictions, and aircraft type. If entries are stale, aircraft entries and conflict-probe notifications may be misleading for both the subject sector and surrounding facilities.

choices.text: Because stale trajectory-model data can make aircraft entries and conflict-probe notifications misleading for both the sector and surrounding facilities.
Because timely entries matter only for keeping the aircraft list visually organized.
Because forecast wind updates are the only data source conflict probe actually uses.
Because interim altitude updates are always optional unless the next sector specifically asks for them.

operations.problem: The explanation begins 'Per 13-1-11,' citing a paragraph location instead of teaching the operational knowledge that delay information must be recorded and may be captured through multiple approved methods — automatic ERAM hold tools or manual facility-approved methods.
operations.source_basis: Delay information must be recorded. Delay information may be automatically recorded via use of the ERAM Hold Data Menu, ERAM Hold View, a hold message, or manually on flight progress strips or facility-approved worksheets, in accordance with the facility-defined standard.

match.question_text: How may delay information be recorded in EDST operations?

replacements.question_text: How may delay information be recorded in EDST operations?
replacements.explanation: Delay information must be recorded and may be captured automatically through the ERAM Hold Data Menu, Hold View, or a hold message, or manually on flight progress strips or facility-approved worksheets, following the facility-defined standard.

choices.text: Automatically through ERAM hold tools or manually on flight progress strips or facility-approved worksheets, in accordance with the facility standard.
Only manually on flight progress strips, because ERAM hold tools do not satisfy the requirement.
Only after the flight exits holding, regardless of whether any approved tool captured the delay.
Only when TMU specifically requests the information.

operations.problem: The explanation begins 'Per 13-1-11,' citing a paragraph location instead of teaching the operational timing rule that automated delay recording occurs when the aircraft is cleared out of hold, not upon entry.
operations.source_basis: When using the ERAM Hold Data Menu or Hold View, delays are automatically recorded when the aircraft is cleared out of hold.

match.question_text: When using ERAM Hold Data Menu or Hold View, when is the delay recorded automatically?

replacements.question_text: When using ERAM Hold Data Menu or Hold View, when is the delay recorded automatically?
replacements.explanation: When using the ERAM Hold Data Menu or Hold View, delays are automatically recorded when the aircraft is cleared out of hold — not upon entering holding. Controllers must adhere to all applicable delay reporting directives and record delay information.

choices.text: When the aircraft is cleared out of hold.
As soon as the aircraft enters holding.
Only when the aircraft checks in with the next sector.
Only after a supervisor manually confirms the hold duration.

operations.problem: The explanation begins 'Per 13-1-12,' citing a paragraph location instead of teaching that the overdue-aircraft notification is based on radar track data, and that while updating the route of flight removes the notification, the overdue-aircraft procedures still apply upon receipt.
operations.source_basis: ESDT overdue aircraft notification is based on radar track data. Updating an aircraft's route of flight will remove the overdue aircraft notification.

match.question_text: What is the ESDT overdue-aircraft notification based on?

replacements.question_text: What is the ESDT overdue-aircraft notification based on?
replacements.explanation: The ESDT overdue-aircraft notification is based on radar track data. Updating an aircraft's route of flight will remove the notification, but the overdue-aircraft procedures required by Chapter 10, Section 3 must still be followed when the notification is received.

choices.text: Radar track data.
Only the aircraft's filed route and estimated times over fixes.
Voice position reports received from the pilot on frequency.
The most recent manual entry in the aircraft-list remarks field.

operations.problem: The stem begins 'Under 13-1-13' and the explanation cites 'Per 13-1-13a,' teaching paragraph-location trivia instead of the operational rule that GPD trajectory depictions are authorized for situational awareness and strategic planning only.
operations.source_basis: Graphic depictions of flight trajectories may be used only to aid in situational awareness and strategic planning.

match.question_text: Under 13-1-13, what is the authorized use of the Graphic Plan Display?

replacements.question_text: What is the authorized use of the Graphic Plan Display?
replacements.explanation: Graphic depictions of flight trajectories from the GPD may be used only to aid situational awareness and strategic planning. The GPD is not authorized for radar identification, position information, transfer of radar identification, radar separation, correlation, or point outs.

choices.text: To aid situational awareness and strategic planning.
To replace radar track position for normal separation work.
To provide primary altitude confirmation when Mode C is unavailable.
To conduct point outs and transfer radar identification when workload is high.

operations.problem: The stem begins 'Under 13-1-13' and the explanation cites 'Per 13-1-13c,' teaching paragraph-location trivia instead of the operational prohibition against substituting trajectory-based altitude for Mode C altitude confirmation.
operations.source_basis: Do not use trajectory-based altitude in lieu of Mode C for altitude confirmation.

match.question_text: Under 13-1-13, what may not be used in lieu of Mode C for altitude confirmation?

replacements.question_text: What must not be used in lieu of Mode C for altitude confirmation?
replacements.explanation: Trajectory-based altitude from the GPD must not be used in lieu of Mode C for altitude confirmation. The GPD is authorized only to aid situational awareness and strategic planning.

choices.text: Trajectory-based altitude.
An approved altimeter readout from the aircraft data block.
A pilot-reported altitude obtained over the frequency.
The assigned altitude in the flight plan.

operations.problem: The true/false stem begins 'Under 13-1-13' and the explanation states only 'That statement appears directly in 13-1-13d,' providing no educational value beyond source citation. The tested content — the list of prohibited GPD uses — is already covered comprehensively by flashcard ecaa6221. Additionally, 13-1-13b (trajectory-based positions must not substitute for radar track position) is a distinct prohibition that lacks independent question coverage.
operations.source_basis: Do not use trajectory-based positions as a substitute for radar track position.

match.question_text: Under 13-1-13, the GPD may not be used for radar identification, transfer of radar identification, radar separation, correlation, or point outs.

replacements.question_text: What must trajectory-based positions from the Graphic Plan Display not be used as a substitute for?
replacements.explanation: The Graphic Plan Display may only be used to aid situational awareness and strategic planning. Trajectory-based positions from the GPD must not be used as a substitute for radar track position, and trajectory-based altitude must not be used in lieu of Mode C for altitude confirmation.

choices.text: Radar track position.
Mode C altitude readout.
The filed flight plan route.
Pilot position reports.

operations.problem: The explanation begins 'Per 13-1-14,' citing a paragraph location instead of teaching the operational contingency that conflict probe and trial planning remain in use without forecast winds, but the controller must recognize that alert and trajectory data may be affected.
operations.source_basis: In the event that current forecast wind data are not available, continue use of conflict probe and trial planning with appropriate recognition that alert and trajectory data may be affected.

match.question_text: If current forecast wind data are unavailable, how should conflict probe and trial planning be handled?

replacements.question_text: If current forecast wind data are unavailable, how should conflict probe and trial planning be handled?
replacements.explanation: When current forecast wind data are unavailable, controllers continue using conflict probe and trial planning but must recognize that alert and trajectory data may be affected.

choices.text: Continue using them, recognizing that alert and trajectory data may be affected.
Stop using both tools completely until forecast wind data are restored.
Use conflict probe only, because trial planning becomes prohibited without forecast winds.
Rely on the alert output without qualification, because missing forecast winds affect only display cosmetics.

operations.problem: This knowledge_check activity is a near-duplicate of question d99f0a64. Both ask why automation entries must be timely, both use multiple-choice with a single correct answer, and both test the identical operational concept. The activity adds no additional retrieval mode, operational context, or distinct educational target.
operations.source_basis: The question d99f0a64 already tests this concept. The activity duplicates the same stem ('Why must the sector team perform automation entries in a timely manner?') and the same correct answer with only cosmetic wording differences in the distractors.

content.question_text: Why must the sector team perform automation entries in a timely manner?

choices.text: Because stale trajectory-model data can make aircraft entries and conflict-probe notifications misleading for the sector and surrounding facilities.
Because timely entries are needed only to keep the aircraft list visually organized, not to support probe accuracy.
Because forecast wind updates are the only data source conflict probe actually depends on.
Because interim altitude updates are always optional unless requested by the next sector.

content.explanation: Conflict-probe results depend on current modeled trajectory data, so late automation entries can mislead both the sector and surrounding facilities.

operations.problem: This knowledge_check activity is a near-duplicate of question 80caac66. Both ask 'How may delay information be recorded in EDST operations?' with identical correct answers and substantially overlapping distractor concepts. The activity provides no distinct educational value.
operations.source_basis: The question 80caac66 already tests this concept with identical stem and answer. The activity's choices are reworded versions of the same distractor ideas without adding operational scenario or context.

content.question_text: How may delay information be recorded in EDST operations?

choices.text: Automatically through ERAM hold tools or manually on flight progress strips or facility-approved worksheets, in accordance with the facility standard.
Only manually on flight progress strips, because ERAM hold tools do not satisfy delay-recording requirements.
Only after the flight exits holding, regardless of whether any automated tool captured the delay.
At the controller's discretion, because delay information is optional unless TMU requests it.

content.explanation: Delay information must be recorded, and the paragraph allows both automatic ERAM hold-tool recording and approved manual methods.

operations.problem: This knowledge_check activity is a near-duplicate of question 55c645d5. Both ask what the GPD is allowed or authorized to do, both use multiple-choice, and both test the identical operational concept. The activity's slightly broader distractor wording does not distinguish it as a separate retrieval target.
operations.source_basis: The question 55c645d5 already tests this concept. The activity asks 'What is the Graphic Plan Display allowed to do operationally?' — the same question as the MCQ — and offers no scenario, procedural context, or distinct retrieval mode.

content.question_text: What is the Graphic Plan Display allowed to do operationally?

choices.text: Aid situational awareness and strategic planning only.
Serve as a substitute for radar track position and Mode C when the display looks reliable.
Provide radar identification, radar separation, and point-out approval if the trajectory line is current.
Replace the radar display during temporary surveillance uncertainty.

content.explanation: GPD is limited to situational-awareness and strategic-planning support; it is not used as a substitute for radar track or altitude data or for radar functions.

operations.problem: This knowledge_check activity is a near-duplicate of question fc4f8a95.
